edges.quote: Once his mourning for his wife and son had run its course, the king moved swiftly to resolve the longsimmering issue of the succession. Disregarding the precedents set by King Jaehaerys in 92 and the Great Council in 101, Viserys declared his daughter, Rhaenyra, to be his rightful heir, and named her Princess of Dragonstone.
edges.note: Aemma's death in childbed and the infant Baelon's death a day later remove Viserys's last hope of a son; he names Rhaenyra heir once mourning passes.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: text frames the declaration as the king's immediate next move once mourning ran its course, no intervening decisional beat.

edges.quote: The next occurred at High Tide after Ser Laenor’s funeral, when king and court made the journey to Driftmark to bear witness at his pyre, many on the backs of their dragons.
edges.note: Laenor's funeral is what brings the royal family, the Velaryon boys, and every dragon in the realm together at Driftmark, setting the stage for the brawl.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the funeral gathering is the precondition for the children being together at High Tide, not the cause of the brawl itself (a free choice by young Aemond).

edges.quote: Hold, Strong. First pay the debt you owe me.
edges.note: Aemond invokes the eye Lucerys took from him at Driftmark as the debt he means to collect at Storm's End, moments before pursuing Luke into the storm.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the old wound is Aemond's stated grievance/disposition driving his decision to demand blood, not a mechanical cause of the dragon fight.

edges.quote: “Was it one of your eyes he took, or one of your balls?” Maris asked the prince, in tones sweet as honey.
edges.note: Maris's jeer, after Aemond is turned away without bloodshed, is the immediate goad that sends him after Lucerys once outside Lord Borros's roof.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Maris's taunt is the proximate provocation for Aemond's decision to mount Vhagar and pursue Arrax, a free choice rather than a forced mechanical result.

edges.quote: died without waking. His body was discovered by a serving man at the hour of the bat, when it was the king’s custom to take a cup of hippocras. The servant ran to inform Queen Alicent, whose apartments were on the floor below
edges.note: The servant who finds the king's body reports directly and only to Queen Alicent, setting the concealment in motion within minutes of the death.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the discovery flows straight into Alicent's council with no decisional gap (the text explicitly frames the report-to-Alicent-alone as instructed in advance).

edges.quote: Drawing his dagger, the Clubfoot drew it across his palm. “A blood oath,” he urged, “to bind us all together, brothers unto death.” And so each of the conspirators slashed their palms and clasped hands with one another, swearing brotherhood.
edges.note: The oath is Larys Strong's act within the same all-night council session that conceals Viserys's death and resolves to crown Aegon; it is a beat inside that hub, not a separate event.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: verified the existing node is specifically the oath-swearing moment, not the whole concealment-and-succession-plotting session, which needed its own anchor (green-council-conceals-death-of-viserys, minted here).

edges.quote: The bells began to ring on the tenth day of the third moon of 129 AC, tolling the end of a reign. Grand Maester Orwyle was at last allowed to send forth his ravens, and the black birds took to the air by the hundreds, spreading the word of Aegon’s ascension to every far corner of the realm.
edges.note: The green council's all-night planning session (secrecy, arrests, alliances) culminates directly in the public announcement and coronation of Aegon. [S204 adjudication: CAUSES->ENABLES — Aegon resisted; Cole's threat mediated]
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the coronation is the council's own collective decision executed through preparation, not a further free choice by an outside third party.

edges.quote: The child had not been due for another turn of the moon, but the tidings from King’s Landing had driven the princess into a black fury, and her rage seemed to bring on the birth, as if the babe inside her were angry too, and fighting to get out.
edges.note: News of Aegon's crowning reaching Dragonstone brings on Rhaenyra's early, fatal labor.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the text states the tidings directly brought on the premature birth, an immediate physiological effect with no decisional step between them.

edges.quote: her pain. “She was my only daughter, and they killed her. They stole my crown and murdered my daughter, and they shall answer for it.”
edges.note: Rhaenyra's own vow over her dead daughter (framed as both crown-theft and child-murder) is her stated motive for answering with her own coronation and war.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Rhaenyra's grief and fury is a personal disposition driving her subsequent free decision to crown herself, not a mechanical cause.

edges.quote: They stole my crown and murdered my daughter, and they shall answer for it.
edges.note: Aegon's crowning in King's Landing is answered directly by Rhaenyra convening her own council and, the next day, her own coronation. [S204 adjudication: re-quoted to the crown-theft vow line — grounds usurpation->counter-coronation directly]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon's coronation does not force Rhaenyra's counter-coronation, it is the provocation her own council freely chooses to answer with a rival crowning.

edges.quote: A hasty coronation was held the next day. The arrival of Ser Steffon Darklyn, late of Aegon's Kingsguard, was an occasion of much joy on Dragonstone, especially when it was learned that he and his fellow loyalists (“turncloaks,” Ser Otto would name them, when offering a reward for their capture) had brought the stolen crown of King Jaehaerys the Conciliator.
edges.note: Darklyn's defection from the Kingsguard delivers the stolen crown of Jaehaerys the Conciliator, the very crown Daemon places on Rhaenyra's head.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the stolen crown makes the specific ceremony possible as staged (an ancestral crown, not an improvised one) but does not force the coronation, which the black council had already resolved on.

edges.quote: “We have friends in the riverlands,” the prince said, “though not all of them dare show their colors yet. We need a place where they can gather, a toehold on the mainland large enough to house a sizable host, and strong enough to hold against
edges.note: At the same Dragonstone council that crowns Rhaenyra, Daemon argues the blacks need a mainland toehold for their scattered riverland sympathizers and proposes Harrenhal.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): the council's strategic need is Daemon's stated reason for the assault, which he then personally chooses to lead — a free decision, not a forced mechanical consequence.

edges.quote: “Find riders to master Silverwing, Vermithor, and Seasmoke, and we will have nine dragons against Aegon’s four. Mount and fly their wild kin, and we will number twelve, even without Stormcloud,”
edges.note: Rhaenys's dragonseed strategy, laid out at Rhaenyra's black council, is the war plan that later leads riders to be sought for the wild dragons around Dragonstone, including Sheepstealer.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the council's decision to seek riders for wild/unclaimed dragons is a precondition for Nettles later taming Sheepstealer, but does not force that specific outcome (a free act by a specific rider, chapters later).

edges.quote: It was decided that Jace, being the older of the two, would take the longer, more dangerous task, flying first to the Eyrie to treat with the Lady of the Vale, then to White Harbor to win over Lord Manderly, and lastly to Winterfell to meet with Lord Stark.
edges.note: The black council's decision to send Jacaerys as envoy is what puts him in the Eyrie's High Hall to win Lady Jeyne Arryn's allegiance.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: being sent as envoy is the precondition for the negotiation; Jeyne Arryn's actual choice to ally is her own, so the council's decision does not force the outcome.

edges.quote: gave the queen a stronghold in the heart of Westeros, to which her supporters could rally... and Rhaenyra had many such in the lands watered by the Trident. When Prince Daemon sent forth his call to arms, they rose up all along the rivers, knights and men-at-arms and humble peasants who yet remembered the Realm’s Delight, so beloved of her father, and the way she smiled and charmed them as she made her progress through the riverlands in her youth.
edges.note: The bloodless fall of Harrenhal gives Daemon a rallying point, and his call to arms from that captured seat brings the riverlands up for Rhaenyra en masse.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: text states plainly that the captured stronghold plus the call to arms produced the mass rising, real causation through the intermediate step of Daemon's summons rather than a third party's independent choice.

edges.quote: As the black council sat to consider how to strike back, a raven arrived from Harrenhal. “An eye for an eye, a son for a son,” Prince Daemon wrote. “Lucerys shall be avenged.”
edges.note: Daemon's raven vowing a son-for-a-son reprisal for Luke's death is the direct order that sets Blood and Cheese's mission in motion.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Luke's death does not mechanically cause the murder; Daemon's free decision to demand vengeance mediates, and a specific downstream decision-event (the murder) exists, so the event-target form is preferred over collapsing agency into CAUSES.

edges.quote: the hulking swordsman slew Prince Jaehaerys, striking off the boy’s head with a single blow
edges.note: Blood is the one who strikes the killing blow.
edges.rationale: Role edge to give the new event hub its perpetrator; mirrors the existing blood-butcher KILLS jaehaerys-targaryen-son-of-aegon-ii dyad.

edges.quote: Cheese appeared to snatch up Maelor
edges.note: Cheese is Blood's accomplice who guides the pair into the castle, seizes Maelor, and forces Helaena's choice.
edges.rationale: Role edge for the second perpetrator, completing the hub alongside blood-butcher.

edges.quote: the hulking swordsman slew Prince Jaehaerys, striking off the boy’s head with a single blow
edges.note: Prince Jaehaerys, Aegon II's heir, is the boy killed.
edges.rationale: Role edge naming the victim, standard hub-completion pattern.

edges.quote: Queen Helaena kept her calm, it is said.
edges.note: Helaena is present throughout, forced to choose which child dies, and present at Jaehaerys's death; the traumatic aftermath (her scream) is narrated in the same passage.
edges.rationale: WITNESS_IN: she does not act against the killers, she is present and forced to watch/choose — the correct role for the graph's downstream-effect-on-Helaena ask.

edges.quote: In his grief and fury, King Aegon II commanded that all the city's ratcatchers be taken out and hanged, and this was done.
edges.note: Aegon II's grief and fury over his son's murder drives his order to hang every ratcatcher in the city (Cheese's trade), even though Cheese himself was never caught.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: an explicit emotional/political decision by the king in reaction to the murder, not a forced mechanical outcome.

edges.quote: And with his death, the war of ravens and envoys and marriage pacts came to an end, and the war
edges.note: Luke's death is the hinge the text itself names as ending the diplomatic phase and opening the fighting phase of the Dance.
edges.rationale: PART_OF (war containment): a marquee battle-adjacent death explicitly framed by the text as a phase-change within the war.

edges.quote: It was a war marked by stealth, murder, and betrayal as well, a war fought in shadows and stairwells, council chambers and castle yards with knives and lies
edges.note: The Dance's own defining description (a war of stairwells, knives, and stealth murder) is exactly what the Blood and Cheese killing is.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the assassination is a component atrocity of the war, explicitly of the kind the book's own framing passage calls out.

edges.quote: Finally Vermax carried Jacaerys Velaryon to Winterfell, to treat with its formidable young lord, Cregan Stark.
edges.note: Jace's envoy mission continues from the Eyrie and White Harbor on to Winterfell, where the Pact of Ice and Fire is struck with Cregan Stark.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the same mission that won the Vale is what carries Jace onward to Winterfell, but Cregan's agreement is his own free choice, not forced by the earlier leg's success.

new_nodes.name: Viserys names Rhaenyra heir
new_nodes.identity: Grieving the deaths of Queen Aemma and the infant Prince Baelon in childbed, King Viserys I breaks with the precedents of 92 and 101 AC and declares his daughter Rhaenyra his heir, naming her Princess of Dragonstone; hundreds of lords do obeisance and swear to defend her succession.

new_nodes.name: Aemond loses his eye at Driftmark
new_nodes.identity: At Ser Laenor Velaryon's funeral gathering at High Tide, ten-year-old Prince Aemond Targaryen secretly claims the dragon Vhagar; caught and mocked by Rhaenyra's sons as "the Strongs," he is blinded in one eye by Lucerys Velaryon's dagger in the ensuing brawl.

new_nodes.name: Death of Viserys I
new_nodes.identity: King Viserys I Targaryen dies in his sleep in the Red Keep on the third day of the third moon of 129 AC, ending the longest and most peaceful Targaryen reign and setting the Dance of the Dragons in motion.

new_nodes.name: Green council conceals the death of Viserys I
new_nodes.identity: Queen Alicent, Ser Otto Hightower, and the small council convene the night of Viserys's death, conceal his passing from Princess Rhaenyra and the realm, silence dissent (Lord Beesbury is killed), and resolve to crown Aegon before word can reach Dragonstone.

new_nodes.name: Coronation of Aegon II
new_nodes.identity: Ser Criston Cole crowns Alicent's eldest son Aegon II in the Dragonpit ten days after Viserys I's concealed death, the greens' pre-emptive answer to Rhaenyra's disregarded succession.

new_nodes.name: Murder of Prince Jaehaerys
new_nodes.identity: Acting on Prince Daemon's "an eye for an eye, a son for a son" vow of vengeance for Lucerys's death, the hired killers Blood and Cheese infiltrate the Red Keep and behead Aegon II's young heir, Prince Jaehaerys, before Queen Helaena, escalating the Dance into open atrocity.

flags: Row 106 (dragonseed strategy) has no in-scope downstream anchor for the actual recruitment; routed as coronation-of-rhaenyra ENABLES taming-of-sheepstealer-by-nettles (an existing node from a much later chapter) since Rhaenys's speech names Sheepstealer specifically — flag for review if a closer-scoped dragonseed-recruitment node is minted later.

mysaria CONTRACTED_WITH blood-butcher already exists in edges.jsonl (run_id fab-the-blacks-and-the-greens-16-p02-2026-07-09-enrichment) so it was not re-proposed for the new murder-of-prince-jaehaerys hub; consider adding a CONTRACTED_WITH or AGENT_IN edge directly to the new event slug in a follow-up pass so the hub is fully self-contained.

assault-on-harrenhal's existing wiki-derived edge 'DEFEATS: blacks (track_b: Result)' looks directionally backwards (blacks captured Harrenhal, they were not defeated by it) — out of scope to fix here, flagging for a schema-drift/orphan-edge pass.

The 111 AC anniversary tourney where the 'greens'/'blacks' names originate has no dedicated node (tourney-for-king-viserys-is-accession is a different, 106 AC accession tourney); routed the naming-origin quote through the existing greens/blacks faction nodes instead of minting a seventh marquee node, to keep this packet's mint list to the six genuinely load-bearing anchors.

harvest.note: Laena Velaryon at Driftmark: "tall, slender, and surpassingly lovely... with a great mane of silver-gold ringlets that fell down past her waist" — unused physical-description quote for her node.

"Though Viserys I would reign for nine more years, the bloody seeds of the Dance of the Dragons had already been planted, and 120 AC was the year when they began to sprout" — explicit authorial foreshadowing statement, candidate for reference/foreshadowing-events.md.

Corlys Velaryon's ninth Sea Snake voyage returns "enough gold to buy twenty more ships... saffron, pepper, nutmeg, elephants, and bolts of the finest silk" — rich trade/wealth-origin quote for the Velaryon wealth thread, unused here.

Aemond on claiming Vhagar as a boy: "he was so afraid of being caught that he forgot to be frightened of being burned to death and eaten" — good color quote for his node, not used in this causal pass.

dropped_at_adjudication.reason: faction-target MOTIVATES, oath-scene quote weak for faction formation (verifier + orchestrator)
verifier REJECT: quote grounds naming custom, not birth->faction causation
PART_OF dance for coronation: weak quote + non-battle containment; causally wired via spine
